Question: On July 15, 2008, Phys.org published an article about a catastrophe. Find the explosive force of this catastrophe according to Encyclopedia Britannica, then find the name of the US nuclear test that had the same yield. Your answer should only be the last word of the name of the test.

Annotator Metadata.Steps:
1. Search for "phys org archive"
2. Click on the link for https://phys.org/archive
3. Naviage to July 15, 2008
4. Search the articles for an article that mentions "catastrophe"
5. Note the name of the event (Tunguska catastrophe)
6. Search for "Tunguska catastrophe britannica"
7. Click on the link for Tunguska event
8. Locate the explosive force in the article (15 megatons)
9. Search for "us nuclear test 15 megatons"
10. Record the last word of the name of the test in the search results.

Question: The attached file lists accommodations in the resort town of Seahorse Island. Based on the information in this file, which seems like the better available place to stay for a family that enjoys swimming and wants a full house?

Annotator Metadata.Steps:
1. Open the provided PDF.
2. Check Rental Houses. 
3. Check the house with pool. 
4. Check for availability: Shelley's place is the only fit.

Question: How many edits were made to the Wikipedia page on Antidisestablishmentarianism from its inception until June of 2023?

Annotator Metadata.Steps:
1. Search the web for “Antidisestablishmentarianism”.
2. Click the Wikipedia result.
3. Click “View history” to see edits made to the page.
4. Click “500” to view 500 edits on the page at a time.
5. Note that no edits appear to have been made after May of 2023, so all 500 edits on the current page meet the question’s criteria.
6. Click “older 500” to view older edits.
7. Repeat until I reach the end of the revisions, counting how many sets of 500 I passed until reaching the last page.
8. On the last page, Ctrl-F for “cur” and “prev”. These abbreviations appear before every revision, so the number of times they each appear on the page (minus the number of times they each appear in the description at the top) is the number of revisions on this page.
9. Add the number of revisions on the last page (232), to the number from the pages of 500 (5 pages times 500 edits equals 2500) to get the answer, 2732.
